metadata.title: Reconstructing Kairos and Chronos Across Trump’s Twitter/X Corpus: Computational Methods + Rhetorical Critique

abstract: The collective force of ex-U.S. President Donald Trump's tweets/Xs is palpable in American public culture, political discourse, and academic rhetorical criticism. Adopting a critical and computational approach, this essay offers a novel method for the rhetorical analysis of social media-based public address by shifting emphasis from memorable exemplars of Trump's social media discourse to the flow dynamics among those tweets/Xs. Focused on loops of tweets as recursive argument systems, we use a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) Markov Chain analysis to offer insight into the kairotic and chronic patterning of Trump's social media utterances to map more and less stable argument strategies across distinct periods of his first Twitter/X presidency.

paragraphs: Computationally nuanced forms of rhetorical analysis are becoming more and more timely. Digital objects for rhetorical criticism now include the message board (Pfister, 2014;Shepherd, 2020), social media posts (Holmes & Lussos, 2018;Parmelee, 2014), hashtags (Jackson et al., 2020;Woods & McVey, 2016) and memes (Hahner & Woods, 2019;Pfister & Woods, 2016). Tweets (now Xs) frequently exemplify Trump's rhetoric (Mercieca, 2020;Skinnell, 2018). This essay focuses on Donald Trump's social media presence on Twitter (now renamed X), which intensified the platform's reputation as a way "to bypass media gatekeepers, serving as a new source for journalistic content" (Conway et al., 2015). It aims to provide an accessible introduction to machine learning methods as a technique for analyzing digitally mediated public address as a flow: a patterned sequence of topics whose connective logic varies calculably at discrete periods in time. Rendered as detailed visualizations and in detailed narrative accounts of topical flow, we offer a periodized view of shifts and continuities across Trump's digital rhetorical presidency.

We argue that such shifts and continuities reflect kairotic turning points and chronic themes across Trump's presidency, drawing our evidence from the former commander-in-tweet's social media account. We begin from the view that critical (i.e., interpretive and humanistic) and computational (i.e., machine-learning-based) analyses mutually inform and reinforce one another. To that end, we offer a method for analyzing structural aspects of how social media datasets flow grounded in machine learning algorithms, specifically, the MALLET topic model implementation of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (hereafter LDA) (Omizo, 2020). Whereas "flow" refers to the pathway carved by linkages between discrete bits of information (Williams, 2003), LDA is a well-regarded machine-learning approach for generating a userdefined set of topics for a corpus (i.e., a high-volume data set) that allows a critic to enrich their reading with additional context (McCallum, 2012). In other words, flow describes the familiar route that information takes as a sequence of similarly themed tweets/Xs repeats, and LDA is a computational method for tracking the probability or likelihood of these channels. As Stephen Best and Sharon Marcus (2009) explain, this style of reading seeks "what is evident, perceptible, apprehensible in texts; what is neither hidden nor hiding; what, in the geometrical sense, has length and breadth but no thickness, and therefore covers no depth" (p.9), encouraging critics to look for recurring, surface-level formal patterns.

A key affordance of our distant reading technique is that it processes a volume of discourse that would be difficult to achieve with more conventional close-textual analyses; the corpus analyzed here alone comprises several hundred thousand data points (Brown, 2021). Rather than minimizing the need for skilled rhetorical critics, we believe critics are essential to navigating a tricky interpretive process. Models require the critic's phronetic (i.e., contingent/practical) judgments to decide what a given topic represents, determine how many topics to generate, and track a topic's complex relationship to other topics and its occasioning context(s). The first half of this essay reviews the concepts of kairos and chronos, our machine learning method, and explains the "topical loop" as an essential output of LDA Markov Chain analysis. We then move through the Trump Twitter/X corpus, which we organized in seven "periods," each accompanied by a unique visualization of the topic transition structure.

Kairos/Chronos: LDA as a Critical/Computational Approach A well-regarded approach that yields a set of topics by seeing how words cluster, LDA adds to existing computer-assisted methods already used to enhance rhetorical criticism (e.g., Kaufer et al., 2006;Kaufer & Parry-Giles, 2017). Observing how a document or dataset includes different collections of topics enables the critic to assess what large numbers of documents are ostensibly about using an iterative process that often runs over thousands of cycles (McCallum, 2012). Not only does LDA generate a user-defined number of topical categories based on recurring patterns in a document, set of documents, or dataset, but it also enables the model's user to track the relationships between distinct topics (i.e., topical transitions) to identify whether different topics tend to follow each other (i.e., loops) or if a single topic tends to repeat (i.e., self-loops).

More specifically, our use of LDA extends the utility of two rhetorical terms by describing how they function for machine learning-driven analysis: kairos and chronos. Kairos often signifies "acuteness," "contingency," "a sense of time as occasion," the figuration of "time at an episodic point," and a rhetor's ability to seize upon an opportune moment or occasion for speech. Chronos is "the time of history," "time in a sequential line," and "rhetoric's historical contextualization," offering a longitudinal arc to account for rhetorical acts (Foley, 2010). John Gallagher (2020) situates this pair as constitutive of "machine time" and fuses these concepts by aligning the qualitative dimension of data analysis with kairos and its quantitative counterpart with chronos. Extending this theorization, our use of chronos describes the deep time of tweets/Xs or the quantifiable rhythms that formed regular, repetitive intervals from Trump's first post until his 2021 ban. In our analysis, chronos is quantitative and qualitative: it reflects our reliance on machine learning techniques for determining a topic and how such topics depend upon our interpretation of the top-ranked tweets in that topical category to arrive at an overall description. Once mapped from beginning to end, moments along this continuum may emerge as qualitatively distinct data clusters, marking a kairos that alternately interrupts or loosely corresponds to chronic themes.

Even as LDA may help to identify Trump's commonest choices within and across different periods of his presidency, the kinds of conclusions that can be drawn from a machine-learning approach are limited by human interpretation. For instance, Table 1. tracks one topical self-loop (X50) as a kairotic, periodized theme and a chronic, recurring pattern across the corpus. In 2014, well before Trump ascended to the presidency, one topic appeared to center on a small-scale Ebola outbreak near Atlanta, Georgia. From a chronic (i.e., longitudinal) view of the total corpus, however, this topic category (X50, titled "Covid Response") was much more significantly populated by tweets about the Trump White House's briefings on the COVID-19 pandemic. The latter chronic description does not invalidate the theme's contingent, kairotic emergence in 2014. The topic model identified patterns related to the discussion of disease throughout the Trump corpus, and messaging concerning COVID-19 overwhelmingly defined the category. However, the distinction between periodized descriptions and generalized patterns necessitates an interpretive distinction between data at distinct temporal scales, giving rise to exceptions like the manufactured Ebola crisis of 2014. We find such differences generative for rhetorical analysis, as they invite critics to contextualize and nuance why discrepancies appear.

Our understanding of kairos and chronos also relies on Raymond Williams's (2003) concept of flow. Flow describes the characteristic movement enabled by a given media form as users navigate its channels. Rather than fixating on "the rhetorical impact of a particular Tweet (or X), image, or hashtag," flow is about the paths curated by a platform; to draw conclusions about flow from singular examples would be akin to "studying a sandcastle to understand a beach " Faltesek et al., 2023). According to Williams, flow is always unique to the medium: the flow of television is not identical to the flow of a cartridge-based video game, which is not identical to the flow of a social medium -and even here, the flow of Twitter/X must differ from that of related platforms. In studying kairos and chronos from the perspective of flow, we seek a medium-specific account of rhetorical discourse as a liquid constellation, one whose boundaries require the intervention of a skilled critic willing to read topical outputs and connections as distinct depending on whether we are focused on a singular moment of social media or its longitudinal rhythms. It is admittedly beyond the purview of this essay to compare the subtextual process driving the flow of Twitter/X versus other social media. Instead, our analysis works at the level of Twitter's/X's flow to create a profile of Donald Trump's rhetoric before and throughout his presidency.

sections.header_raw: Methods and Mechanization: Supervised Systems Topic Modeling
sections.header_norm: methods and mechanization supervised systems topic modeling
paragraphs: This section reviews the MALLET implementation of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (McCallum, 2002). LDA supposes that every document can be understood as including many topics, which are collections of words (Ganegedara et al., 2020). There are many parameters the critic can assign without guidance about what the optimal numbers might be. Primarily, a critic shapes the model by producing a corpus and assigning a number of topics to find. A powerful form of supervision for the model is the selection of stop words, which the computer should ignore in the analysis. For this study, a standard selection of stop words was used. After an initial run of the model, it was clear that tokens like "t.co" and "rt" interfered with the analysis. A handful of similar tokens were added to the stop words list, and the model was re-run. The results were cleaner and yielded clear exemplars. Although there are conceptual and computational issues with LDA, particularly the lack of rigorous aprioristic justifications for alpha, beta, and topic number assumptions (Gerlach et al., 2018), the base utility of topic modeling systems is their capacity to enhance qualitative reading and analysis through categorization (Evans & Fanelli, 2014. Beyond MALLET, we also used the following software to create our visual images: TidyText, ggplot2, and dplyr (Silge & Robinson, 2022;Wickham et al., 2021Wickham et al., , 2022)). Our step-by-step process was as follows:

(1) Theorize the Cultural Context. We reviewed accounts of the context and significant texts related to the total period under consideration and familiarized ourselves with rhetorical analyses concerning Trump's Twitter/X presidency. (2) Secure the Archive. We located existing datasets, gained API access, transcribed data, and scraped tweets/Xs. Building the best possible archive for the process was more important than finding the archive quickly. (3) Cleaning Data. "Cleaning" refers to exploratory work to remove irrelevant material and noise. This is an essential step but requires care, given that what are irrelevant terms for some may be the most important for others. (4) Topic Modeling. We ran the topic model process described in the preceding sections. If perplexity is used to justify the topic model, it must be run multiple times to discern the optimal topic number. We found this to be a low-labor computational process. This process yielded probability scores that designated certain tweets/Xs as the most exemplary of each topic category. All probable assignments were filtered to remove all tweets with a probability score below 0.5. Such low scores indicate that a topic is not verifiably present. For instance, it struck us as reasonable to delete the low chance that a post promoted a new episode of The Apprentice long after the program had been canceled. (5) Unify the Dataset. We reconnected the archive with the metadata (who/what/ where/when) and topic values, joining the two datasets created in steps 3 and 4.

(6) Assign Topic Names. This was an extensive qualitative process of reading highvalue elements in each category and assigning a name to the category based on an interpretive assessment of those elements and transitions. Each author assigned possible topic names independently before meeting to deliberate about coding. In most cases, we agreed on the most salient topics for each category, which we labeled X1 through X50 (each designated by an "X" followed by a number), with default alpha and beta assumptions and a suitably large arbitrary number of training runs. In cases that we did not, we presented our interpretations to one another and collectively decided upon the most appropriate heading. We offered two titles for each topic: one that captured a more momentary, kairotic content description specific to the period under consideration and another chronic description that captured themes drawn from the most common/highly weighted tweets occurring across the total corpus. Each author also extensively read the highly weighted tweets within each category to validate the topic descriptions. Ultimately, we used approximately three words per topic to help explain what the model had captured in a series of tweets/Xs. (7) Chain Processing. Because low-probability topics were removed, we pivoted from "wide" to "long" data, facilitating our chain processing method. (8) Periodize. Sort the highest probability transitions into "periods" based on the time slices in which tweets were most concentrated (see Figure 5 and Figure 6). A researcher familiar with the context under consideration may be able to make effective choices about how these breaks may be distributed. However, if the researcher desires, other time series methods may be employed in this step, such as searching for seasonal auto-correlations between topics. (9) Read Transitions. Like step 6, this step involved an extensive interpretation of the loops created in any/all periods and their functions (e.g., argument, accusation). In our write-up, colons indicate the direction and sequence of a topical transition. For instance, X13:X37 signifies a transition from the "Rally Bragging" topic (X13) to the "Television Appearances" topic (X37), meaning that topics in the latter category were weighted as likely to follow those in the former. (10) Visualize Markov Chain. Accompanying each period is also a force-directed diagram of the network of tweets/Xs using the Fruchterman-Reingold algorithm (Fruchterman & Reingold, 1991). We recommend any network method that may visualize the transitions under consideration during a given period. However, this may require a different computational background and knowledge than the other steps. Many approaches to visualizing Markov processes depend on many signed edges in a network diagram. We moved from RStudio to Gephi for visualization because the results are much easier to read for anything except the smallest networks (Gephi -The Open Graph Viz Platform, 2022). Network diagrams have their own rhetorical entailments, potentially feeding conspiratorial reasoning if not checked with substantial additional explanation (Tufte, 2006). The graphics presented here are coded in saturation from no relationship as white to a saturated green, depending on intensity. (11) Juxtapose Contexts. Compare and contrast the new context given by the transitions with the cultural context established in Step 1. The literature in

Step 1 established a narrative baseline to compare the empirical data. ( 12) Write/Report Analysis. We explained the juxtaposition of old/new context in a narrative format, offering hypotheses for these accounts' (non-)alignment.

The topic model process was completed successfully. Full analytics are presented in the linked appendix, including the topic words from LDA, various summary means and variances, and human topic derivation. One likely objection is that other implementations of LDA offer slight differences in the progression of steps or tunable parameters. Not only were these models unavailable when we initially sought to publish this essay, but we are also unconvinced that these would produce different results given our extensive interpretative process. Hence, we have decided not to employ sentence-level embeddings, word-vector embeddings, seeded tensor replications, or other advanced computational methods. The benefits of employing such models or techniques are offset by their lack of explanation or unknown functional properties (Heaven, 2024). Our interpretation of the model's results took approximately twenty hours via FaceTime audio calls. We invoke our time deliberating to align our critical and computational approach with Dwight Conquergood's (1991) imperative of locating "fragile constructions" of living discourse, understanding that this is our best means to hold back "nihilism or gutless equivocation." (92). In this case, we consider the pitfalls of nihilism and equivocation closely tied to the chase for computational purity as an alternative to a sharp interpretive reading. In this study, the analysis was the product of a collaborative critical reading of topical clusters situated in the dimensional space of the events widely reported to have produced those tweets/Xs. Methodologically, three choices merit additional explanation. First, we selected fifty discrete topics to privilege explainability. A typical method for justifying a topic model is to calculate the perplexity of the model, which should fall into something of a sweet spot, although selecting this "spot" requires arbitrary human intervention (Zhao et al., 2015). Griffiths and Steyvers (2004) visualize the optimal topic domain as the peak of a parabola in their foundational work on this method. However, accuracy in predicting semantically rich content can be obfuscated by this technique (Dudy & Bedrick, 2020). Fifty topics offered a large, round number that we could cognitively manage, allowing us to keep distinctions between categories interpretable, coherent, and discernible, which Chauhan et al. (2022) maintain as essential criteria (p. 27). Fifty also offered sufficient topical diversity to represent the corpus as distinct, periodized moments. Ultimately, we believe success in any modeling process must be defined qualitatively.

While it might be reasonable to find larger numbers of topics, we also decided upon fewer as a preferable alternative to overfitting. Overfitting occurs when a model is tuned in such a way as to show maximum fit, even if that fit does not reflect reality. It is a real risk when applying increasingly granular models to natural language, where many documents -or single tweets -would appear to merit their own topical category. At its most extreme, seeking the most granular fit possible is nihilism: By assigning every unit of analyzable discourse its own topic, we lose the ability to detect clustered themes, commonalities, or arguments. The result of creating too many topics is to "mistake data that are part of the noise for part of the pattern," overfitting the model to the data in ways that make the latter incomprehensible for a human interpretant (Hayles, 2023, p. 637).

Second, when marking periods across the corpus, we picked seven major world and electoral events corresponding with noteworthy variations in topical density and transitions. Rather than relying solely on the model to determine the corpus's distinct periods, many of our time boundaries correspond with the electoral cycle (e.g., pre-election, post-election, midterms). We also noted politically significant, widely-reported events strongly represented in his presidential messaging (e.g., Hillary Clinton's investigation by the FBI in 2016, the Mueller and Cloudflare investigations, the COVID-19 pandemic, and Trump's first Congressional impeachment). These periods were not always equal in length, nor did they always include a comparable number of topics. The specific start-and end-dates for the corpus correspond with Trump's first and last day on Twitter/X (in the case of Periods 1 and 7 (Figure 1 and 11) or the first/ last day of a given month. Determining these periods requires the interpretive intervention of a critic, as it is ultimately the person and not just the process that makes the temporal cut. Even if other critics opt for broader or narrower periodization, our selection reflects an accumulation of topical transitions that reliably represent a given moment that would have been intelligible to a wide series of publics (e.g., journalists, career politicians, and potential voters) before we applied our analysis.

Finally, approaching the change in topics over time is a well-established methodology that promises to be useful for rhetorical critics looking to couch claims about effectivity and persuasion in an empirically driven view of how social media data flows from one moment to the next. Wang and McCallum (2006) established that LDA might offer a view of the change in topics over time. When accounting for topic duration, some topics tend to be quite peaky, and others persist over time. Semantic shift analysis is also moving from merely detecting shifts to offering rich explanations, deviations around important events, and possible cause-and-effect relationships (Kutuzov et al., 2018;Ngo et al., 2021). Other analyses use a combination of topic and sentiment over time, for instance, to chart the course of the early pandemic, where, at first, people were united and later were at odds with each other (Wicke & Bolognesi, 2021). These kinds of topical changes offer a window into social shifts by sorting units of social media data into representational categories and, more crucially, establishing observable transition dynamics between them (Heyer et al., 2009).

This section reviews the MALLET implementation of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (McCallum, 2002). LDA supposes that every document can be understood as including many topics, which are collections of words (Ganegedara et al., 2020). There are many parameters the critic can assign without guidance about what the optimal numbers might be. Primarily, a critic shapes the model by producing a corpus and assigning a number of topics to find. A powerful form of supervision for the model is the selection of stop words, which the computer should ignore in the analysis. For this study, a standard selection of stop words was used. After an initial run of the model, it was clear that tokens like "t.co" and "rt" interfered with the analysis. A handful of similar tokens were added to the stop words list, and the model was re-run. The results were cleaner and yielded clear exemplars. Although there are conceptual and computational issues with LDA, particularly the lack of rigorous aprioristic justifications for alpha, beta, and topic number assumptions (Gerlach et al., 2018), the base utility of topic modeling systems is their capacity to enhance qualitative reading and analysis through categorization (Evans & Fanelli, 2014. Beyond MALLET, we also used the following software to create our visual images: TidyText, ggplot2, and dplyr (Silge & Robinson, 2022;Wickham et al., 2021Wickham et al., , 2022)). Our step-by-step process was as follows:

(1) Theorize the Cultural Context. We reviewed accounts of the context and significant texts related to the total period under consideration and familiarized ourselves with rhetorical analyses concerning Trump's Twitter/X presidency. (2) Secure the Archive. We located existing datasets, gained API access, transcribed data, and scraped tweets/Xs. Building the best possible archive for the process was more important than finding the archive quickly. (3) Cleaning Data. "Cleaning" refers to exploratory work to remove irrelevant material and noise. This is an essential step but requires care, given that what are irrelevant terms for some may be the most important for others. (4) Topic Modeling. We ran the topic model process described in the preceding sections. If perplexity is used to justify the topic model, it must be run multiple times to discern the optimal topic number. We found this to be a low-labor computational process. This process yielded probability scores that designated certain tweets/Xs as the most exemplary of each topic category. All probable assignments were filtered to remove all tweets with a probability score below 0.5. Such low scores indicate that a topic is not verifiably present. For instance, it struck us as reasonable to delete the low chance that a post promoted a new episode of The Apprentice long after the program had been canceled. (5) Unify the Dataset. We reconnected the archive with the metadata (who/what/ where/when) and topic values, joining the two datasets created in steps 3 and 4.

(6) Assign Topic Names. This was an extensive qualitative process of reading highvalue elements in each category and assigning a name to the category based on an interpretive assessment of those elements and transitions. Each author assigned possible topic names independently before meeting to deliberate about coding. In most cases, we agreed on the most salient topics for each category, which we labeled X1 through X50 (each designated by an "X" followed by a number), with default alpha and beta assumptions and a suitably large arbitrary number of training runs. In cases that we did not, we presented our interpretations to one another and collectively decided upon the most appropriate heading. We offered two titles for each topic: one that captured a more momentary, kairotic content description specific to the period under consideration and another chronic description that captured themes drawn from the most common/highly weighted tweets occurring across the total corpus. Each author also extensively read the highly weighted tweets within each category to validate the topic descriptions. Ultimately, we used approximately three words per topic to help explain what the model had captured in a series of tweets/Xs. (7) Chain Processing. Because low-probability topics were removed, we pivoted from "wide" to "long" data, facilitating our chain processing method. (8) Periodize. Sort the highest probability transitions into "periods" based on the time slices in which tweets were most concentrated (see Figure 5 and Figure 6). A researcher familiar with the context under consideration may be able to make effective choices about how these breaks may be distributed. However, if the researcher desires, other time series methods may be employed in this step, such as searching for seasonal auto-correlations between topics. (9) Read Transitions. Like step 6, this step involved an extensive interpretation of the loops created in any/all periods and their functions (e.g., argument, accusation). In our write-up, colons indicate the direction and sequence of a topical transition. For instance, X13:X37 signifies a transition from the "Rally Bragging" topic (X13) to the "Television Appearances" topic (X37), meaning that topics in the latter category were weighted as likely to follow those in the former. (10) Visualize Markov Chain. Accompanying each period is also a force-directed diagram of the network of tweets/Xs using the Fruchterman-Reingold algorithm (Fruchterman & Reingold, 1991). We recommend any network method that may visualize the transitions under consideration during a given period. However, this may require a different computational background and knowledge than the other steps. Many approaches to visualizing Markov processes depend on many signed edges in a network diagram. We moved from RStudio to Gephi for visualization because the results are much easier to read for anything except the smallest networks (Gephi -The Open Graph Viz Platform, 2022). Network diagrams have their own rhetorical entailments, potentially feeding conspiratorial reasoning if not checked with substantial additional explanation (Tufte, 2006). The graphics presented here are coded in saturation from no relationship as white to a saturated green, depending on intensity. (11) Juxtapose Contexts. Compare and contrast the new context given by the transitions with the cultural context established in Step 1. The literature in

Step 1 established a narrative baseline to compare the empirical data. ( 12) Write/Report Analysis. We explained the juxtaposition of old/new context in a narrative format, offering hypotheses for these accounts' (non-)alignment.

The topic model process was completed successfully. Full analytics are presented in the linked appendix, including the topic words from LDA, various summary means and variances, and human topic derivation. sections.paper_id: Hallsby and Faltesek - 2025 - Reconstructing Kairos and Chronos Across Trump’s TwitterX Corpus Computational Metho
sections.header_raw: Topical Loops and Data Set Periodizations
sections.header_norm: topical loops and data set periodizations
paragraphs: Network analysis defines loops in terms of the likelihood that one data point belonging to a given category (or topic) will be succeeded chronologically by another belonging to the same category (self-loops) or a different one (loops). For example, consider "Figure 1," which depicts Period 1 (Figure 1) of Trump's Twitter/X dataset, comprising his prepresidency from when he first joined Twitter/X in 2009 until January 1, 2013. There, the use of Twitter/X focuses on a few nodes, particularly those involved in his self-promotion, which are centered in the diagram. Two topics were disproportionately featured during this period: television appearances (X37), appearing 3 times in the top 12 topical transitions of this period, and attacks on President Barack Obama (X30), which appeared ten times in the same list. His self-promotional rhetoric offers at least one example of a loop in which successive tweets transition between distinct topics. The most common state transition during this period concerns attacks on Obama (X30), and the X30:X30 transition is the most highly weighted, nearly double any other loop during this period. This is a self-loop: one attack on Obama was often followed by another tweet/X from the same topic category. Other loops include X6:30, which signals a transition from discussions of oil prices to attacks on Obama, and X26:X30, in which discussions of Middle East peace transition to attacks on Obama. One additional observation gathered from this period is that there is not a single tweet before the 2011 White House Correspondents dinner, lending credence to the popular theory that this event set Trump's Twitter/X presidency in motion (Roberts, 2016). Transition loops mark each period in two ways: those that accumulate as a unique signature of the moment (i.e., kairos) and those that display recurring patterns across the total corpus (i.e., chronos). In Period 1 (Figure 1) (2011-2013: "Early Trump Twitter"), Trump's celebrity persona is characterized by ongoing topic variation around a deep pool of his self-image. The ways he talked about Obama and his selfpromotional processes were varied, and his positive affirmations and negative attacks collided in an almost random pattern. Thursday was Apprentice night, and the chronological logic of the television schedule created a cadence effect where Trump's tweets always circled back toward self-aggrandizing positivity. Before he began to define himself against Obama, he was seldom locked into a single loop.

Period 2 (Figure 2) [2013-15: "Up to the Announcement that Trump was Running"] and 3 [2015-16: "From Declaration to Election"] represent Trump's peak Twitter/X use, which is also why they are divided in our scheme. Loops around his celebrity identity and claims to audience response animated a larger-than-life constellation of media effects. The world of current events begins to load new topic ideas into the world, burning briefly and brightly before falling away. Jeb Bush was crushed by his feedback loop, his request to "Please Clap" offering profound evidence of his rhetorical failures (Please Clap | Know Your Meme, 2016).

It is here also that our topics begin to register Trump's overtures to what Sarah Jackson and Kreiss (2023) call "defensive publics," or those that "stand in alignment with (rather than counter to)" the entrenched white supremacy of a hegemonic public sphere, seeking "to preserve the ordering of systems of inequality" (p. 103). Whereas Figure 2 manifests these appeals as attacks on Obama's birthright citizenship (X30), in Figure 3, such attacks fall under the heading of "Law and Order" (X31) and "Zimmerman and Ferguson" (X43). In later Figures 45), this trend is most visible under the topic heading of "Border Wall Racism" (X18).

Period 4 (Figure 4) [2016-18: "Election until Midterm"] captures a turning point as Trump's prominent self-talk was replaced by a greater concentration of attacks against Hillary Clinton, which continued to intensify after the election's conclusion. Some of the prominent tweet topics during this period reflect stock issues of presidential address, such as disaster response, commemoration, and commentary on the economy. However, attacks remain the most prominent feature of this period as more actors launched attacks on behalf of the incoming President. The intensity of outwardly directed attacks and slowing topical changes became more prominent as new ways of talking about others replaced how Trump talked about himself. However, as attacks on Clinton failed to win the 2018 midterm election, they were replaced by the older strategy of attacking Obama.

Period 5 (Figure 7) [2018-19: "Post-Midterm and First Impeachment"] depicts the aftermath of the 2018 midterm elections. There are still issue-based moments but in a profoundly reactive mode. Presidential aesthetics melt away, with fewer and fewer commemorative moments highlighting heroic or exceptional Americans. Concomitantly, Trump's impeachment defense moved toward more abstract and diffuse attacks on the Democratic congresspeople. As the COVID-19 pandemic arrives in Period 6 (Figure 8) [January-November 2020: "The Pandemic Election"], President Trump is still mired in reactive defenses against impeachment while attempting to manage multiple public crises. Notably, the pandemic enabled him to speak to the American public for hours daily, perhaps explaining why the network in this period was extremely noisy. Trump's direct attacks on Biden were delayed into August, overlapping with the nomination of Amy Coney Barrett as Supreme Court Justice. Unlike earlier moments, this period is characterized by reactive topic shifts. Contrary to the views of Trump as a master rhetor who created false exigencies via social media, this period shows a Trump beholden to the events as they transpired.

Finally, in Period 7 (Figure 11) [November 2020-January 2021: "Post-Election Blues"], we see only one driving, continuous attack: election fraud. Although this is the shortest period, it is dominated by a conspiracy-driven discourse resembling a single continuous bellow, replacing earlier patterns of layered voices and multitopic loops. Thus assembled, our approach to Trump's social media flow foregrounds the shifting, connective logic that articulates broad categories of social media text to one another. In the following analysis, we offer a more in-depth chronological account of Trump's 2016-2020 Twitter/X presidency, seeking to place our observations in conversation with relevant rhetorical scholarship and provide a step-by-step analysis that increases in complexity and depth as it progresses from one period to the next.

Network analysis defines loops in terms of the likelihood that one data point belonging to a given category (or topic) will be succeeded chronologically by another belonging to the same category (self-loops) or a different one (loops). For example, consider "Figure 1," which depicts Period 1 (Figure 1) of Trump's Twitter/X dataset, comprising his prepresidency from when he first joined Twitter/X in 2009 until January 1, 2013. There, the use of Twitter/X focuses on a few nodes, particularly those involved in his self-promotion, which are centered in the diagram. Two topics were disproportionately featured during this period: television appearances (X37), appearing 3 times in the top 12 topical transitions of this period, and attacks on President Barack Obama (X30), which appeared ten times in the same list. His self-promotional rhetoric offers at least one example of a loop in which successive tweets transition between distinct topics. The most common state transition during this period concerns attacks on Obama (X30), and the X30:X30 transition is the most highly weighted, nearly double any other loop during this period. This is a self-loop: one attack on Obama was often followed by another tweet/X from the same topic category. Other loops include X6:30, which signals a transition from discussions of oil prices to attacks on Obama, and X26:X30, in which discussions of Middle East peace transition to attacks on Obama. One additional observation gathered from this period is that there is not a single tweet before the 2011 White House Correspondents dinner, lending credence to the popular theory that this event set Trump's Twitter/X presidency in motion (Roberts, 2016). Transition loops mark each period in two ways: those that accumulate as a unique signature of the moment (i.e., kairos) and those that display recurring patterns across the total corpus (i.e., chronos). In Period 1 (Figure 1) (2011-2013: "Early Trump Twitter"), Trump's celebrity persona is characterized by ongoing topic variation around a deep pool of his self-image. The ways he talked about Obama and his selfpromotional processes were varied, and his positive affirmations and negative attacks collided in an almost random pattern. Thursday was Apprentice night, and the chronological logic of the television schedule created a cadence effect where Trump's tweets always circled back toward self-aggrandizing positivity. Before he began to define himself against Obama, he was seldom locked into a single loop.

Period 2 (Figure 2) [2013-15: "Up to the Announcement that Trump was Running"] and 3 [2015-16: "From Declaration to Election"] represent Trump's peak Twitter/X use, which is also why they are divided in our scheme. Loops around his celebrity identity and claims to audience response animated a larger-than-life constellation of media effects. The world of current events begins to load new topic ideas into the world, burning briefly and brightly before falling away. Jeb Bush was crushed by his feedback loop, his request to "Please Clap" offering profound evidence of his rhetorical failures (Please Clap | Know Your Meme, 2016).

It is here also that our topics begin to register Trump's overtures to what Sarah Jackson and Kreiss (2023) call "defensive publics," or those that "stand in alignment with (rather than counter to)" the entrenched white supremacy of a hegemonic public sphere, seeking "to preserve the ordering of systems of inequality" (p. 103). Whereas Figure 2 manifests these appeals as attacks on Obama's birthright citizenship (X30), in Figure 3, such attacks fall under the heading of "Law and Order" (X31) and "Zimmerman and Ferguson" (X43). In later Figures 45), this trend is most visible under the topic heading of "Border Wall Racism" (X18).

Period 4 (Figure 4) [2016-18: "Election until Midterm"] captures a turning point as Trump's prominent self-talk was replaced by a greater concentration of attacks against Hillary Clinton, which continued to intensify after the election's conclusion. Some of the prominent tweet topics during this period reflect stock issues of presidential address, such as disaster response, commemoration, and commentary on the economy. However, attacks remain the most prominent feature of this period as more actors launched attacks on behalf of the incoming President. The intensity of outwardly directed attacks and slowing topical changes became more prominent as new ways of talking about others replaced how Trump talked about himself. However, as attacks on Clinton failed to win the 2018 midterm election, they were replaced by the older strategy of attacking Obama.

Period 5 (Figure 7) [2018-19: "Post-Midterm and First Impeachment"] depicts the aftermath of the 2018 midterm elections. There are still issue-based moments but in a profoundly reactive mode. Presidential aesthetics melt away, with fewer and fewer commemorative moments highlighting heroic or exceptional Americans. Concomitantly, Trump's impeachment defense moved toward more abstract and diffuse attacks on the Democratic congresspeople. As the COVID-19 pandemic arrives in Period 6 (Figure 8) [January-November 2020: "The Pandemic Election"], President Trump is still mired in reactive defenses against impeachment while attempting to manage multiple public crises. Notably, the pandemic enabled him to speak to the American public for hours daily, perhaps explaining why the network in this period was extremely noisy. Trump's direct attacks on Biden were delayed into August, overlapping with the nomination of Amy Coney Barrett as Supreme Court Justice. Unlike earlier moments, this period is characterized by reactive topic shifts. Contrary to the views of Trump as a master rhetor who created false exigencies via social media, this period shows a Trump beholden to the events as they transpired.

Finally, in Period 7 (Figure 11) [November 2020-January 2021: "Post-Election Blues"], we see only one driving, continuous attack: election fraud. Although this is the shortest period, it is dominated by a conspiracy-driven discourse resembling a single continuous bellow, replacing earlier patterns of layered voices and multitopic loops. Thus assembled, our approach to Trump's social media flow foregrounds the shifting, connective logic that articulates broad categories of social media text to one another. In the following analysis, we offer a more in-depth chronological account of Trump's 2016-2020 Twitter/X presidency, seeking to place our observations in conversation with relevant rhetorical scholarship and provide a step-by-step analysis that increases in complexity and depth as it progresses from one period to the next.

paragraphs: In Period 2 (Figure 2) (2013-15: "Up to the Announcement that Trump was Running"), the most salient pattern consists of Trump's continuous recirculation of positive appraisals of his popularity. The top thirteen most highly weighted topics are all self-loops, signaling a consistency of social media repetition in which a topic is invoked only to be followed by a subsequent invocation of the same topic. Apart from his responses to a four-person Ebola virus outbreak in 2014 (X50), his speech does not respond in real-time to contingent or emergent events. Instead, the documented transitions show that he repeats a similar, relatively invariant formula. Trump pits himself against the Democrats, especially in reference to Benghazi. Selfloops predominate, with the most high-weighted topics including endorsements (X2), Benghazi (X21), self-praise (X46), law and order (X31, which is in this period pegged to the phrase "stop-and-frisk"), and domestic policy (X34). His attacks are framed around foreign/domestic policy and crime. This method of reflecting on the news provides him with copious content with no obligation for responsible invention. Additionally, Obama's disappearance from such attacks makes sense, given that his most likely Democratic opponent, Hillary Clinton, remained captured by Republican efforts to relitigate the 2012 Benghazi scandal that occurred during her term as Secretary of State.

This period also illustrates how general topic descriptions (i.e., chronos) are mismatched with periodized descriptions (i.e., kairos). Highly weighted occurrences of topic categories whose chronic descriptors include "Courts" (X43) and "COVID Briefings" (X47) may seem unsensible or ill-fitting in this period. However, as shown in Table 2, the kairotic descriptions of these categories are more sensible and reflect time bounded topics [i.e., "Zimmerman and Ferguson" (X43)] or Trump's self-celebration [i.e., "Praise for Trump" (X47)]. Other topic headings, such as appeals to "law and order" (X31), remain consistent across chronic and kairotic periodizations. Attacks on Democrats and foreign policy eventually morph into attacks on a specific Democrat: Hillary Clinton. Although "fake news" is not a prominent topic at this early stage, the kernel of this argument is already in place. Rather than seeing the campaign as something that began with his 2015 declaration of intent to run for office, topic modeling visualizes the campaign as a process that unfolded over several years, morphing as Trump moved from insurgent nominee to establishment incumbent. Period 3 (Figure 3) begins just after Trump's famous June 6, 2015 "escalator speech," where he declared his intent to run for President. During this period, there is a striking continuity between kairotic (periodized) and chronic (longitudinal) topical descriptions. Self-loops still occupy the first through fifth highest-weighted positions, foremost among them "Rally Bragging" (X13). This topic tended to boast about the size of Trump's crowds as evidence of his popular support. Other prominent self-loops included "Attacks on Republicans" (X23)
and "Attacks on Hillary Clinton, Bernie Sanders, and Elizabeth Warren" (X16), reflecting a similar pattern of name-calling and taunting directed at Republican and Democratic opponents alike.

One unignorable aspect of the social media activity late in this period concerns the role of foreign electoral interference on Twitter/X. According to Kathleen Hall Jamieson (2020), Russian troll farms exercised a disproportionate influence over message amplification during this period, working "feverishly to focus the media agenda on Clinton's vulnerabilities and away from Trump's" (161). This was most especially the case in October 2016, which included such events as the October 7 release of Trump's compromising Access Hollywood tape, the release of hacked e-mail content from the Democratic National Committee (DNC), October 9 television interviews that featured the hacked content rather than the Access Hollywood leak, three Trump/Clinton debates, and finally FBI Director James Comey's announcement of an investigation into Hillary Clinton based on the hacked DNC e-mails. In confirmation of these observations, our findings show that self-looping "Attacks on Hillary Clinton" (X5) are fifth-highest weighted in Period 3 (Figure 3), while in Period 4 (Figure 4) ["Election (2016) until Midterm (2018)] the most highly weighted loop was concerned the chronic "Witch Hunt" (X1) topic, which in kairotic, periodspecific terms primarily captured the John Podesta hack, the FBI investigation into Hillary Clinton, and accusations of Russian electoral interference. This finding may be novel and surprising because it locates a heavier concentration of attacks on Hillary Clinton's campaign after the election, and there is no differentiation between such attacks and Trump's efforts to deny the corruption of his electoral campaign.

A further sign of relative sophistication in the pre-election Twitter/X strategy concerns non-identical topic transitions, which occupy the sixth through tenth most highly weighted positions and signal greater variation in topic sequencing compared to Periods 1-2 (Figure 1 and 2).

As shown in Table 3, we note dyadic and triangular patterns between topics X37 and X13, as well as a triangular pattern that includes X8. Whereas a dyadic loop alternates between two topics, triangular loops bear three distinct topical points. Both variations create circular warrant structures that allow earlier arguments to function as evidence for later ones. For instance, "Television Appearances" (X37) links to "Rally Bragging" (X13), which links to "2016 Polling" (X8), which subsequently links back to "Television Appearances" (X37). Such loops work together by using earlier claims to create a highly selfreferential argument structure. They are also distinct from those that come earlier and later in his presidency (e.g., Period 7 (Figure 11): Post-Election Blues), which instead display single, self-looping arguments that repeat the same claim ad infinitum.

Period 4 (Figure 4) [Election (Nov. 2016) to Midterm (Nov. 2018)] marks a shift as Trump embraced more standard Presidential topics alongside his usual strategy of scapegoating perceived adversaries. In the aftermath of the 2016 election, Twitter/X displays less topic shifting and more reliance on topics like "Eulogy and Memorialization" (X11), "Natural Disaster Response" (X5), and "Foreign Policy" (X45). Topics like "Natural Disaster Response" (X5) are chronic across the corpus and consistently highly weighted. However, the sudden salience of this topic is speculatively attributable to Trump's formal role in responding to natural disasters and an escalating mid-Atlantic hurricane season beginning in 2017 (Murakami et al., 2018).

At times, Donald Trump's rhetoric has been assumed to reflect a purposeful rhetorical strategy, whether by "intentionally subverting the dominant meanings of key words by using dog whistles to appeal to their partisans" or by using ad hominem arguments and paraliptic sentence structures (Mercieca 280). As shown in Table 4, many highly-ranked repetitive single-claim self-loops support this thesis as Trump's tweets/Xs gravitated toward 2016 election conspiracy theories (X1), speculations about the U.S. economy's Table 3 Period 3 strength (X3), attacks on foreign trade and outsourced manufacturing (X19), and "Border Wall Racism" (X18), the last of which captures fear-mongering about migrant caravans at the U.S.-Mexico border (X18). However, beyond his intentional invocation of specific topics, Period 4 (Figure 4) also illuminates how Trump's ascendancy to the presidency changed his social media use as he began to adopt topics (e.g., eulogy/memorialization) more aligned with stock issues invoked regularly by earlier Presidents (Campbell & Jamieson, 2008, p. 6).

Figures 5 and 6 visualize how, in Period 4 (Figure 4), Trump fell into a pattern of less concentrated social media use. His most topically dense/diverse social media days occurred during his pre-campaign (Period 2 (Figure 2)), after he declared his intent to run (Period 3 (Figure 3)), and from 2019 until 2020, which coincided first with his impeachment and then with the start of the COVID-19 pandemic (Period 5 (Figure 7)). In Period 4 (Figure 4), however, Trump's Twitter/X volume fell precipitously compared to the periods before and after. As he entered office (Period 4 (Figure 4)), his Twitter/X hewed closely to a limited number of stock topics: "Witch Hunt" (X1), "Border Wall Racism" (X18), "Endorsements" (X2), and "China/Foreign Labor" (X19).

Perhaps the most abundant rhetorical scholarship about Donald Trump concerns his recurring patterns of self-victimization comprised alternately of "a drama about precarity rather than vulnerability" (Johnson, 2017, p. 231) and "appeals to rage, malice, and revenge" (Kelly, 2020, p. 3), both of which kept his "supporters in a perpetual state of anger" (Bostdorff, 2017, p. 698). Whereas much of this criticism has attended to discourse Table 4 Period 4. Endorsements 53 4 4 X18:X18 Migrant Caravan Border Wall Racism 43 5 5 X45:X45 Meeting Leaders Foreign Policy 41 6 6 X41:X41 Made-up Stories / Lies / Failing NYT / Inaccurate Coverage / Fake News Fake News 38 7 7 X5:X5 Thoughts and Prayers / FEMA / Monitoring the Situation Natural Disasters 35 8 8 X11:X11 Thank you for your service / Today we Remember and Celebrate Eulogy/Memorialization 29 9 X1:X41 Election Interference to Fake News Trump's flow primarily concentrates on self-looping tweets/Xs about "Impeachment Denial" (X10) and "Attacks on his Democrats" (X36), as well as transitions between these topics. Such attack-and-defense departs from earlier strategies, marking a reactive, period- Figure 6 Identical to Figure 5, with added vertical bars to approximate our (kairotic) periodizations Figure 7 Topic Transitions for Period 5, Nov. 2018-Jan.2020: "Midterm Until Pandemic"

specific kairos. Attacks previously associated with Clinton (77 mentions) or Obama (97 mentions) are now more broadly directed to Democrats (566 mentions) and House Majority Leader Nancy Pelosi (150 mentions), which speculatively correspond with the agencies of his impeachment. Democrats also figure prominently across other categories like "Impeachment Denial" (X10), "Attacks on Democrats" (X80), "Border Wall Racism" (X18), and "Attacks on Leaders" (X27).

From a more chronic perspective, several landmark events that cut across Periods 4 and 5 (Figures 4 and 7) are also visible as topical continuities concerning foreign policy and the Middle East. These include Trump's meeting with Vladimir Putin in Oslo on July 16, 2018, the murder of Jamal Khashoggi on October 2, 2018, and a second meeting between Trump and Putin in Argentina during the G20 Summit between November 29-30, 2018. From a longitudinal point of view, the weight assigned to "Foreign Policy" (X45) remains relatively stable, most often referencing anticipatory or follow-up statements about Trump's meetings with foreign leaders. However, Figure 5 also alludes to a subtle departure from this trend, as "Middle East Foreign Policy Statements" (X26) now overshadow the more general "Foreign Policy" (X45) topic category.

Period 6 (Figure 8) depicts the time slice between the announcement of COVID-19 in the United States and the 2020 presidential election. It reflects three prevailing topical clusters: (1) COVID-19 response, (2) Trump's campaign messaging, and (3) a continuation of the previous period's impeachment defense. The campaign messaging cluster dominates Figure 5 and is comprised of six self-looped topics: "Endorsements" (X2:X2, ranked 1) and "Election Fraud" (X46:X46, ranked 3) are the most prominent while "Attacks on Biden" (X29:X29, ranked 9), "Trump's 2020 Campaign Argument" (X17:X17, ranked 13), "Attacks on Leaders" (X27:X27, ranked 16), and "Rally Bragging" (X13:X13, ranked 19) fall substantially lower. The centrally-located connection between X2, X27, and X29 may be attributable to the fact that the dominant target in this period is Biden (398 mentions), while the topic that contains the most focused version of a campaign argument (X17) is substantially weaker and falls to the periphery of the network, falling to the side in comparison to Trump's reactions to emerging events (see Figure 9).

Although the (2) campaign messaging and (3) anti-impeachment defense clusters are more highly ranked than (1) COVID-19 response, this cluster ["Attacks on N.Y. Politicians" (X34), "COVID Response" (X50), and "COVID Briefings" (X47)] has a notable triangular structure and topic density. These topics self-loop and are connected by lower-ranking but prominent topical transitions (e.g., X34:X50, ranked 14 th ; X50:X34, ranked 18 th ).

Figure 10 isolates several noteworthy self-loops from Period 6 (Figure 8), including "Law and Order" (X31), "Attacks on N.Y. Politicians/COVID Relief" (X34), "Fake News" (X41), and "Judicial Appointments" (X43). Clustered according to the dates when they were most prominent, the chart shows the relative compression of these topics, which reference emergent, kairotic events. "Law and Order" (X31), for instance, is clustered around the months during and after George Floyd's murder and Derek Chauvin's arraignment. COVID Relief (X34) is present across the timeline as a continuous rather than static exigency. As the impeachment fades, "Fake News" (X41) also declines in topic density. Finally, as Amy Coney Barrett's nomination begins, "Judicial Appointments (X43) surges.

Period 7 (Figure 11) marks the end of Trump's presidency. Viewed solely in terms of the sparseness of topics, it bears greatest resemblance to Period 1 (Figure 1). Covering a significantly shorter timespan than the others, Figure 11 reflects low topic density and visible single lines, comprising only 267 transitions from November 1, 2020, until Trump's Twitter/X ban in January 2021. The network is concentrated around election conspiracies (X46). In the first 33 topics of the category, X46 appears 23 times. Self-loops account for 19.4% of the topics, while 56.1% of all topic transitions link to X46. Prominent tweets in X46 voice suspicion of absentee ballots and voting machines set up in heavily Democratic areas, as well as accusations of disloyalty levied against members of the GOP, particularly in Georgia. These tweets also claim thousands of "found" ballots that necessitate a recount and accusations that the election was "rigged" against Trump. Of the handful of non-conspiracy connections, only a few trailing Endorsements and All Lives Matter arguments remain. Only 11% of the remaining activity is coronavirus-related, and 44% of that traffic is inbound or outbound from the conspiracy core. The rich loops previously present have been replaced with one argument: the conspiracy theory that the election was stolen.

This was most especially the case in October 2016, which included such events as the October 7 release of Trump's compromising Access Hollywood tape, the release of hacked e-mail content from the Democratic National Committee (DNC), October 9 television interviews that featured the hacked content rather than the Access Hollywood leak, three Trump/Clinton debates, and finally FBI Director James Comey's announcement of an investigation into Hillary Clinton based on the hacked DNC e-mails. In confirmation of these observations, our findings show that self-looping "Attacks on Hillary Clinton" (X5) are fifth-highest weighted in Period 3 (Figure 3), while in Period 4 (Figure 4) ["Election (2016) until Midterm (2018)] the most highly weighted loop was concerned the chronic "Witch Hunt" (X1) topic, which in kairotic, periodspecific terms primarily captured the John Podesta hack, the FBI investigation into Hillary Clinton, and accusations of Russian electoral interference. This finding may be novel and surprising because it locates a heavier concentration of attacks on Hillary Clinton's campaign after the election, and there is no differentiation between such attacks and Trump's efforts to deny the corruption of his electoral campaign.

A further sign of relative sophistication in the pre-election Twitter/X strategy concerns non-identical topic transitions, which occupy the sixth through tenth most highly weighted positions and signal greater variation in topic sequencing compared to Periods 1-2 (Figure 1 and 2).

As shown in Table 3, we note dyadic and triangular patterns between topics X37 and X13, as well as a triangular pattern that includes X8. Whereas a dyadic loop alternates between two topics, triangular loops bear three distinct topical points. Both variations create circular warrant structures that allow earlier arguments to function as evidence for later ones. For instance, "Television Appearances" (X37) links to "Rally Bragging" (X13), which links to "2016 Polling" (X8), which subsequently links back to "Television Appearances" (X37). Such loops work together by using earlier claims to create a highly selfreferential argument structure. They are also distinct from those that come earlier and later in his presidency (e.g., Period 7 (Figure 11): Post-Election Blues), which instead display single, self-looping arguments that repeat the same claim ad infinitum.

Period 4 (Figure 4) [Election (Nov. 2016) to Midterm (Nov. 2018)] marks a shift as Trump embraced more standard Presidential topics alongside his usual strategy of scapegoating perceived adversaries. In the aftermath of the 2016 election, Twitter/X displays less topic shifting and more reliance on topics like "Eulogy and Memorialization" (X11), "Natural Disaster Response" (X5), and "Foreign Policy" (X45). Topics like "Natural Disaster Response" (X5) are chronic across the corpus and consistently highly weighted. However, the sudden salience of this topic is speculatively attributable to Trump's formal role in responding to natural disasters and an escalating mid-Atlantic hurricane season beginning in 2017 (Murakami et al., 2018).

At times, Donald Trump's rhetoric has been assumed to reflect a purposeful rhetorical strategy, whether by "intentionally subverting the dominant meanings of key words by using dog whistles to appeal to their partisans" or by using ad hominem arguments and paraliptic sentence structures (Mercieca 280). As shown in Table 4, many highly-ranked repetitive single-claim self-loops support this thesis as Trump's tweets/Xs gravitated toward 2016 election conspiracy theories (X1), speculations about the U.S. economy's Table 3 Period 3 strength (X3), attacks on foreign trade and outsourced manufacturing (X19), and "Border Wall Racism" (X18), the last of which captures fear-mongering about migrant caravans at the U.S.-Mexico border (X18). However, beyond his intentional invocation of specific topics, Period 4 (Figure 4) also illuminates how Trump's ascendancy to the presidency changed his social media use as he began to adopt topics (e.g., eulogy/memorialization) more aligned with stock issues invoked regularly by earlier Presidents (Campbell & Jamieson, 2008, p. 6).

Figures 5 and 6 visualize how, in Period 4 (Figure 4), Trump fell into a pattern of less concentrated social media use. His most topically dense/diverse social media days occurred during his pre-campaign (Period 2 (Figure 2)), after he declared his intent to run (Period 3 (Figure 3)), and from 2019 until 2020, which coincided first with his impeachment and then with the start of the COVID-19 pandemic (Period 5 (Figure 7)). In Period 4 (Figure 4), however, Trump's Twitter/X volume fell precipitously compared to the periods before and after. As he entered office (Period 4 (Figure 4)), his Twitter/X hewed closely to a limited number of stock topics: "Witch Hunt" (X1), "Border Wall Racism" (X18), "Endorsements" (X2), and "China/Foreign Labor" (X19).

Perhaps the most abundant rhetorical scholarship about Donald Trump concerns his recurring patterns of self-victimization comprised alternately of "a drama about precarity rather than vulnerability" (Johnson, 2017, p. 231) and "appeals to rage, malice, and revenge" (Kelly, 2020, p. 3), both of which kept his "supporters in a perpetual state of anger" (Bostdorff, 2017, p. 698). Whereas much of this criticism has attended to discourse Table 4 Period 4. Democrats also figure prominently across other categories like "Impeachment Denial" (X10), "Attacks on Democrats" (X80), "Border Wall Racism" (X18), and "Attacks on Leaders" (X27).

From a more chronic perspective, several landmark events that cut across Periods 4 and 5 (Figures 4 and 7) are also visible as topical continuities concerning foreign policy and the Middle East. These include Trump's meeting with Vladimir Putin in Oslo on July 16, 2018, the murder of Jamal Khashoggi on October 2, 2018, and a second meeting between Trump and Putin in Argentina during the G20 Summit between November 29-30, 2018. From a longitudinal point of view, the weight assigned to "Foreign Policy" (X45) remains relatively stable, most often referencing anticipatory or follow-up statements about Trump's meetings with foreign leaders. However, Figure 5 also alludes to a subtle departure from this trend, as "Middle East Foreign Policy Statements" (X26) now overshadow the more general "Foreign Policy" (X45) topic category.

Period 6 (Figure 8) depicts the time slice between the announcement of COVID-19 in the United States and the 2020 presidential election. It reflects three prevailing topical clusters: (1) COVID-19 response, (2) Trump's campaign messaging, and (3) a continuation of the previous period's impeachment defense. The campaign messaging cluster dominates Figure 5 and is comprised of six self-looped topics: "Endorsements" (X2:X2, ranked 1) and "Election Fraud" (X46:X46, ranked 3) are the most prominent while "Attacks on Biden" (X29:X29, ranked 9), "Trump's 2020 Campaign Argument" (X17:X17, ranked 13), "Attacks on Leaders" (X27:X27, ranked 16), and "Rally Bragging" (X13:X13, ranked 19) fall substantially lower. The centrally-located connection between X2, X27, and X29 may be attributable to the fact that the dominant target in this period is Biden (398 mentions), while the topic that contains the most focused version of a campaign argument (X17) is substantially weaker and falls to the periphery of the network, falling to the side in comparison to Trump's reactions to emerging events (see Figure 9).

Although the (2) campaign messaging and (3) anti-impeachment defense clusters are more highly ranked than (1) COVID-19 response, this cluster ["Attacks on N.Y. Politicians" (X34), "COVID Response" (X50), and "COVID Briefings" (X47)] has a notable triangular structure and topic density. These topics self-loop and are connected by lower-ranking but prominent topical transitions (e.g., X34:X50, ranked 14 th ; X50:X34, ranked 18 th ).

Figure 10 isolates several noteworthy self-loops from Period 6 (Figure 8), including "Law and Order" (X31), "Attacks on N.Y. Politicians/COVID Relief" (X34), "Fake News" (X41), and "Judicial Appointments" (X43). Clustered according to the dates when they were most prominent, the chart shows the relative compression of these topics, which reference emergent, kairotic events. "Law and Order" (X31), for instance, is clustered around the months during and after George Floyd's murder and Derek Chauvin's arraignment. COVID Relief (X34) is present across the timeline as a continuous rather than static exigency. As the impeachment fades, "Fake News" (X41) also declines in topic density. Finally, as Amy Coney Barrett's nomination begins, "Judicial Appointments (X43) surges.

Period 7 (Figure 11) marks the end of Trump's presidency. Viewed solely in terms of the sparseness of topics, it bears greatest resemblance to Period 1 (Figure 1). Covering a significantly shorter timespan than the others, Figure 11 reflects low topic density and visible single lines, comprising only 267 transitions from November 1, 2020, until Trump's Twitter/X ban in January 2021. The network is concentrated around election conspiracies (X46). In the first 33 topics of the category, X46 appears 23 times. Self-loops account for 19.4% of the topics, while 56.1% of all topic transitions link to X46. Prominent tweets in X46 voice suspicion of absentee ballots and voting machines set up in heavily Democratic areas, as well as accusations of disloyalty levied against members of the GOP, particularly in Georgia. These tweets also claim thousands of "found" ballots that necessitate a recount and accusations that the election was "rigged" against Trump. Of the handful of non-conspiracy connections, only a few trailing Endorsements and All Lives Matter arguments remain. Only 11% of the remaining activity is coronavirus-related, and 44% of that traffic is inbound or outbound from the conspiracy core. The rich loops previously present have been replaced with one argument: the conspiracy theory that the election was stolen.

paragraphs: This essay's main contribution is to make a case for LDA analysis for rhetorical criticism while acknowledging that such methods are not novel in an absolute sense. For rhetorical critics who might balk at the use of machine learning methods to elaborate concepts like kairos and chronos, we would argue that more conventional Figure 10 The x-axis tracks the date within Period 6, and the y-axis tracks topic assignment density. Topic descriptions for each of the x-values appear in Table 5
Table 5 Period 6: "Pandemic (Jan 2020) until Election" (Nov 2020) Legend for Figure 10

Self-Loop Rank Overall Rank Loop Periodized Content Description(Kairos) General ContentDescription(Chronos) Weight 5 5 X34:X34 COVID Response Attacks on N.Y. Politicians/COVID Relief 94 6 6 X31:X31 George Floyd Law and Order 75 10 10 X43:X43 Judicial Appointments Judicial Appointments 34 12 12 X41:X41 Fake News Fake News

forms of rhetorical analysis -for instance, those that would center a single speech or even a fragmented series of discourses -are less and less feasible when the object of analysis is a social media corpus. It is impractical for an individual critic -or even a team of critics -to comb through data to locate recurring themes because the unwieldy bigness of datasets like the one considered here requires tools capable of navigating ever-larger volumes.

LDA and other forms of machine learning are harmonious with more conventional forms of rhetorical analysis in at least one fundamental respect: they enable the critic to move bidirectionally from text to context and from context to text. To understand how a situation informs the creation of a text, our method considers emergent crisis-like circumstances as initial explanations for why flows of discourse unfold on social media. As a movement from text to context, our topic model organizes a large volume of discourse to enable critics to assess whether these discursive productions may be constitutive of the very crises to which they respond. In other words, our use of LDA elaborates how a day, a month, or a year of tweets/ Xs is a kind of argument that seeks to make one issue more exigent than others cooccurring alongside it. A further tension navigated by the preceding analysis concerns the toggle between our reliance on machine learning outputs (i.e., allowing the machine to steer the analysis) and a human-driven approach that reduces machine outputs to entirely subjective content summaries (i.e., allowing our preconceived opinions about Trump's Twitter/X presidency to drive our interpretations). The former risk is associated with the problem of overfitting that also plagues A.I. systems, which produces outputs that appear quantitatively valid but bear little resemblance to the chaotic, living discourse of the social media flow. The latter may be associated with the problem of overdetermination, in which an interpretant's ideology or biases hypostatize the meaning of a topic in ways that make machine learning analysis superfluous or ornamental.

Although it is beyond the scope of this essay to resolve this aporia, we conclude by noting how the so-called problem of interpretative bias is ineradicable, meaning that the rhetorical critic will always be a factor of analysis. With respect to overfitting, we made an interpretive determination about where to draw the cut with respect to the number of topics. With respect to overdetermination, the summative titles assigned to each topic depended upon each of our independent assessments concerning what LDA had captured, subsequently confirmed or revised based on incongruities between our respective observations. It was our decision, for instance, to divide the corpus into seven discrete sections, just as it was also our choice to select 50 topics, with some more coherent than others. This essay's distinction between kairos and chronos testifies to this subjective imprint, as topical generalizations appear to hold less and less as periods become smaller, more granular, and more reflective of a specific moment.

By electing to study Trump's famous social media account as a series of units in rapid succession, we have also offered an alternative approach to rhetorical criticism. What was prominent for Trump's Twitter/X did not reflect what was widely regarded as the most critical issue, topic, or trend of the day, but rather a reciprocal feedback loop by which Trump ingested the news and put it through a digestive cycle that sought more and less successfully to steer where it might go next. To rethink social media posting as an alternating flow of kairos and chronos requires dealing with not just the relative timeliness of single or multiple statements. Moderation of such a flow of arguments does not function on the level of the post but the entire flow. Developing counter-argumentative strategies and fact-checks for individual posts misses the context inherent to loops that provided robust evidence for some publics. Such an approach can help understand other contexts, including subreddits, stitches/duets on TikTok, locative posts on Snapchat, and conversation flow on Discord. Empirical work requires dealing with the evolving and dynamic nature of the loop as it connects to similar and different statements. Moving forward, there is much more to be done along the lines presented here: while more sophisticated language models offer different clusters of discourse, datasets associated with landmark events could help us to further disentangle effects from causes-and larger panels of authors may arrive at crisper critiques, our analysis here emphasizes that Trump's power was derived from his Twitter/X, not his tweets/Xs.

forms of rhetorical analysis -for instance, those that would center a single speech or even a fragmented series of discourses -are less and less feasible when the object of analysis is a social media corpus. It is impractical for an individual critic -or even a team of critics -to comb through data to locate recurring themes because the unwieldy bigness of datasets like the one considered here requires tools capable of navigating ever-larger volumes.

LDA and other forms of machine learning are harmonious with more conventional forms of rhetorical analysis in at least one fundamental respect: they enable the critic to move bidirectionally from text to context and from context to text. To understand how a situation informs the creation of a text, our method considers emergent crisis-like circumstances as initial explanations for why flows of discourse unfold on social media. As a movement from text to context, our topic model organizes a large volume of discourse to enable critics to assess whether these discursive productions may be constitutive of the very crises to which they respond. In other words, our use of LDA elaborates how a day, a month, or a year of tweets/ Xs is a kind of argument that seeks to make one issue more exigent than others cooccurring alongside it. A further tension navigated by the preceding analysis concerns the toggle between our reliance on machine learning outputs (i.e., allowing the machine to steer the analysis) and a human-driven approach that reduces machine outputs to entirely subjective content summaries (i.e., allowing our preconceived opinions about Trump's Twitter/X presidency to drive our interpretations). The former risk is associated with the problem of overfitting that also plagues A.I. systems, which produces outputs that appear quantitatively valid but bear little resemblance to the chaotic, living discourse of the social media flow. The latter may be associated with the problem of overdetermination, in which an interpretant's ideology or biases hypostatize the meaning of a topic in ways that make machine learning analysis superfluous or ornamental.

Although it is beyond the scope of this essay to resolve this aporia, we conclude by noting how the so-called problem of interpretative bias is ineradicable, meaning that the rhetorical critic will always be a factor of analysis. With respect to overfitting, we made an interpretive determination about where to draw the cut with respect to the number of topics. With respect to overdetermination, the summative titles assigned to each topic depended upon each of our independent assessments concerning what LDA had captured, subsequently confirmed or revised based on incongruities between our respective observations. It was our decision, for instance, to divide the corpus into seven discrete sections, just as it was also our choice to select 50 topics, with some more coherent than others. This essay's distinction between kairos and chronos testifies to this subjective imprint, as topical generalizations appear to hold less and less as periods become smaller, more granular, and more reflective of a specific moment.

By electing to study Trump's famous social media account as a series of units in rapid succession, we have also offered an alternative approach to rhetorical criticism. What was prominent for Trump's Twitter/X did not reflect what was widely regarded as the most critical issue, topic, or trend of the day, but rather a reciprocal feedback loop by which Trump ingested the news and put it through a digestive cycle that sought more and less successfully to steer where it might go next. To rethink social media posting as an alternating flow of kairos and chronos requires dealing with not just the relative timeliness of single or multiple statements. Moderation of such a flow of arguments does not function on the level of the post but the entire flow. Developing counter-argumentative strategies and fact-checks for individual posts misses the context inherent to loops that provided robust evidence for some publics. Such an approach can help understand other contexts, including subreddits, stitches/duets on TikTok, locative posts on Snapchat, and conversation flow on Discord. Empirical work requires dealing with the evolving and dynamic nature of the loop as it connects to similar and different statements. Moving forward, there is much more to be done along the lines presented here: while more sophisticated language models offer different clusters of discourse, datasets associated with landmark events could help us to further disentangle effects from causes-and larger panels of authors may arrive at crisper critiques, our analysis here emphasizes that Trump's power was derived from his Twitter/X, not his tweets/Xs.
